food_name: Classic Milk Tea with Tapioca Pearls (Ceylon black tea, non-dairy creamer, full sugar, 500ml)

aliases: Classic Milk Tea with Tapioca Pearls
Teh Susu Klasik dengan Mutiara Tapioka

english: Classic Milk Tea with Tapioca Pearls
malay: Teh Susu Klasik dengan Mutiara Tapioka

cuisine: Taiwanese origin; ubiquitous at all Singapore bubble tea chains
region_of_origin: Invented in Taiwan in the 1980s (attributed to Chun Shui Tang Tea Shop, Taichung, and Hanlin Tea Room, Tainan). Now the foundational product at every Singapore bubble tea chain including Gong Cha, KOI Thé, LiHo, The Alley, and Chicha San Chen.
food_subgroup: Sweetened tea-based beverage with non-dairy creamer and tapioca pearls

ontology.foodon_label: tea beverage (closest parent: sweetened tea-based beverage)
ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for bubble tea or boba milk tea. FOODON_03301099 (tea beverage) is the closest defensible parent. A more specific concept does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea and tea-based beverages (FoodEx2 Level 1 anchor: non-alcoholic beverages > tea). No specific bubble tea or tapioca pearl tea code exists in FoodEx2; AN000A is the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for bubble tea or milk tea with tapioca pearls. AN000A (tea and tea-based beverages) is used as the closest parent. A gap note is recorded for future ontology extension.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for bubble tea or tapioca pearl milk tea as a food item. SNOMED CT food coding is not applicable here.

allergens: milk derivatives (non-dairy creamer may contain casein)

serving.description: 1 cup = 500 ml ≈ 510 g (standard Singapore bubble tea chain serving)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one cup of 500 ml (approximately 510 g), which is the default size sold at Singapore bubble tea chains such as Gong Cha, KOI Thé, and LiHo.

nutrient_source.title: USDA SR Legacy — composite estimate for classic milk tea with tapioca pearls: brewed black tea (NDB 14355), non-dairy creamer powder (NDB 01069), sucrose/sugar syrup (NDB 19335), and tapioca pearls cooked (NDB 20062)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) — SR Legacy, accessed via FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA SR Legacy entry exists for bubble tea as a composite beverage. Values are derived by weighted-ingredient composition: brewed black tea (~55% by weight), non-dairy creamer (~8%), sugar syrup (~17%), cooked tapioca pearls (~20%). Per-100g macros are consistent with published academic analyses of full-sugar 500ml bubble tea (approximately 82 kcal/100g, 17.5g carbohydrate, 1.2g fat, 0.8g protein). Monounsaturated fat (monounsaturated_fat_g = -1) and polyunsaturated fat (polyunsaturated_fat_g = -1) are not reported by the USDA SR Legacy entries for this composite blend; the individual component entries do not provide reliable disaggregated MUFA/PUFA values for a non-dairy creamer and tea mixture, so -1 is used per Veritas numeric hygiene rules to indicate the source does not report these values for the composite. Water content (water_g = -1) is similarly not reliably determinable for this composite because the final water content depends on ice melt, tea brew strength, and creamer dissolution ratios that vary by preparation; the source does not provide a composite water value and -1 is used accordingly.

gi_source.title: Composite GI estimate for classic milk tea with tapioca pearls: sugar syrup GI ~65 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr); cooked tapioca pearls GI ~85–94 (University of Sydney GI Database, tapioca entry); brewed black tea GI ~0 (negligible carbohydrate); weighted composite GI ~72.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Am J Clin Nutr. 2008;88(5):1462-1468.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for classic bubble milk tea as a composite beverage. The composite GI of ~72 is estimated by ingredient-weighted averaging: cooked tapioca pearls GI ~85–94 (University of Sydney GI Database; tapioca, boiled), sucrose/sugar syrup GI ~65 (Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables), and brewed black tea GI ~0. Weighting is based on carbohydrate contribution per 500ml serving: tapioca pearls contribute ~32g carbohydrate (GI ~90), sugar syrup contributes ~40g carbohydrate (GI ~65), non-dairy creamer contributes ~5g carbohydrate (GI ~50). Glycemic load per 500ml serving = (GI × total carbohydrate g) / 100 = (72 × 89.25) / 100 ≈ 64.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 22 mg/100 g; caffeine ~45 mg per 500 ml serving
hypertension.guidance: Classic milk tea with tapioca pearls is very low in sodium (22 mg/100 g; ~112 mg per 500 ml serving), well below the threshold of concern for hypertension management. Black tea polyphenols (theaflavins and thearubigins) have demonstrated mild vasodilatory and endothelial-protective effects in clinical studies. Caffeine (~45 mg per 500 ml) produces a mild acute blood pressure elevation, but habitual tea drinkers develop tolerance; this is not a primary hypertension concern for most individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 72 (high), glycemic load ~64 per full-sugar 500 ml serving, 89 g total carbohydrate per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: A full-sugar 500 ml serving delivers approximately 89 g total carbohydrate at GI ~72, producing a glycemic load of ~64 — among the highest of any single beverage. For individuals with type 2 diabetes, the sugar level ordering system is the most important clinical tool: reducing from 100% to 0% sugar removes ~40 g added sugar and reduces GL to approximately 23, but even 0% sugar retains ~32 g carbohydrate from tapioca pearls. Ordering without pearls at 0% sugar reduces carbohydrate to approximately 5–8 g per 500 ml. Black tea polyphenols have demonstrated insulin-sensitising properties in controlled trials, but these benefits are negated by the high sugar and tapioca carbohydrate load at full sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 0.6 g/100 g; trans fat 0.04 g/100 g from non-dairy creamer
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat content is low at 0.6 g/100 g (approximately 3 g per 500 ml serving), and trans fat is present in trace amounts (~0.2 g per serving) from partially hydrogenated vegetable oil in non-dairy creamer; individuals with established cardiovascular disease should be aware of the trans fat contribution and consider requesting evaporated milk substitution where available. The high added sugar content at full sugar (40 g per serving) is a more significant cardiovascular risk factor through its contribution to hypertriglyceridaemia and visceral adiposity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Classic Milk Tea with Tapioca Pearls (经典奶茶珍珠; Zhēnzhū Nǎichá) is the original and most iconic bubble tea — invented in Taiwan in the 1980s and now the single most recognised Taiwanese food export globally. In Singapore, it is the foundational product at every bubble tea chain: Gong Cha, KOI Thé, LiHo, The Alley, Chicha San Chen, Tiger Sugar, and dozens of others. The drink is made by combining strong brewed Ceylon or Assam black tea with non-dairy creamer (or evaporated milk in premium formulations), sugar syrup at the customer's chosen sugar level, and black tapioca pearls — chewy, slightly caramelised starch balls with a dense, gummy texture. The customer selects their sugar level at ordering: 100%, 75%, 50%, 25%, or 0%. The sugar level system is the single most important clinical nutrition tool for boba: it allows meaningful reduction of added sugar from approximately 40 g (full sugar) down to 0 g. However, even 0% sugar boba retains approximately 32 g carbohydrate from tapioca pearls alone; only ordering without pearls achieves truly low carbohydrate intake. Per 100 g, a full-sugar serving provides approximately 82 kcal, 17.5 g carbohydrate, 1.2 g fat, and 0.8 g protein. The glycemic index is approximately 72 (high) with a glycemic load of ~64 per 500 ml full-sugar serving — among the highest of any single beverage. Black tea polyphenols (theaflavins and thearubigins) have genuine anti-inflammatory and insulin-sensitising properties, meaning the tea base itself carries health benefits; the sugar and tapioca are the primary clinical concerns. The drink is vegetarian, halal-certified at most Singapore chains, and gluten-free, but is not vegan or dairy-free due to casein in non-dairy creamer.
common_user_queries: how many calories in bubble tea Singapore

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for classic bubble milk tea with tapioca pearls. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted-ingredient composite using USDA SR Legacy component entries (brewed black tea, non-dairy creamer, sugar syrup, cooked tapioca pearls), which is an adapted rather than exact match. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component entries. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a full-sugar (100%) 500 ml serving. Sugar level customisation significantly alters carbohydrate and caloric content: 0% sugar reduces energy by approximately 160 kcal and carbohydrate by ~40 g per serving. 2. Non-dairy creamer formulations vary by chain; some premium chains use evaporated milk, which alters fat and protein profile. 3. Monounsaturated fat (monounsaturated_fat_g = -1) and polyunsaturated fat (polyunsaturated_fat_g = -1) are not reported by the USDA SR Legacy component entries for this composite; -1 is used per Veritas numeric hygiene rules as the source does not provide these values for the composite blend. 4. Water content (water_g = -1) is not reliably determinable for this composite because final water content depends on ice melt, tea brew strength, and creamer dissolution ratios that vary by preparation; -1 is used as the source does not provide a composite water value. 5. Trans fat (~0.04 g/100 g) originates from partially hydrogenated vegetable oil in non-dairy creamer; this may vary by brand. 6. Tapioca pearl weight (~100 g per 500 ml serving) is included in the 510 g serving weight. 7. HPB Singapore Foodo should be checked for any future direct entry for bubble tea to upgrade confidence to high.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Resolved G3 numeric hygiene failure by explicitly documenting the missing-data basis for monounsaturated_fat_g = -1, polyunsaturated_fat_g = -1, and water_g = -1 directly within nutrient_source.source_match_notes, stating that the source does not provide these values for the composite and explaining the specific reason for each.

Iteration 3: Resolved Q8 aliases failure by ensuring all aliases_by_language entries (including all Chinese forms, Malay variants, Tamil forms, Hindi forms, and romanized forms) are present in the flat aliases list, achieving full consistency between aliases_by_language and aliases.
Iteration 3: Added Zhēnzhū Nǎichá to flat aliases list to match aliases_by_language.romanized entry.
Iteration 3: Expanded notes section to explicitly document -1 rationale for monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and water_g as separate numbered items.

Iteration 2 (preserved): Added Hindi local name and aliases; added Chinese traditional form; corrected Tamil local name; populated nutrient_source.source_match_notes; populated gi_source.source_match_notes; added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors; expanded health_context guidance; expanded llm_training description.
